apps.usage: College algebra students reflect on their exam performance, identifying patterns in their mistakes and developing specific strategies for improvement. The tool turns a disappointing grade into a learning opportunity with a concrete path forward.
apps.impact: Turned exam failure from a dead end into a turning point — students who used to see a bad grade as confirmation they "can't do math" started seeing it as diagnostic information about exactly what they need to practice next.

apps.name: KIPP NOLA Passage Generator
apps.description: KIPP New Orleans teachers generate reading passages that match the rigor and format of Louisiana's LEAP assessment.
apps.usage: KIPP New Orleans teachers generate reading passages that match the rigor and format of Louisiana's LEAP assessment. The tool ensures students practice with texts that prepare them for what they'll actually encounter on test day.
apps.impact: Ensured KIPP NOLA students aren't surprised by the LEAP — they've practiced with passages that match the real thing in complexity, format, and question style.

apps.name: NYS K-5 Question Set Generator
apps.description: Creates question sets or pathways in the moment for teachers to guide students through problem-solving processes based on their misconceptions, emphasizing open-ended exploration and critical thinking.
apps.creator: Beri Tamsen
apps.usage: K-5 math teachers use this app during instruction to quickly generate targeted question sets when they notice students struggling with a concept. Rather than relying on pre-planned interventions, teachers can respond in real time by pulling up question pathways that guide students from their specific misconception toward deeper understanding through open-ended exploration.
apps.impact: Teachers are becoming more responsive in the moment, shifting from telling students what they got wrong to asking questions that help students discover the reasoning gap themselves. The emphasis on critical thinking over correct answers has changed the culture of math classrooms, with students more willing to take risks and share their thinking because mistakes have become launching points rather than dead ends.

apps.name: NYCPS Mathematics Shifts Coaching Tool
apps.description: Supports math coaches in NYCPS as they navigate the districtwide shifts in math instruction alongside the teachers they coach. Coaches can analyze low-inference observation notes, plan coaching meetings aligned to district criteria for success, and monitor for teacher practice growth.
apps.creator: NYC Solves
apps.usage: Math coaches in NYCPS use this tool to analyze classroom observation notes against district criteria for success, plan targeted coaching meetings, and track teacher growth over time. The app aligns all coaching work to the districtwide mathematics shifts, ensuring consistency and coherence across the coaching support teachers receive.
apps.impact: Coaching across the district is becoming more aligned and evidence-based, with coaches able to connect individual teacher feedback to the broader vision for math instruction in NYCPS. The structured approach has helped coaches move from reactive support to proactive, data-informed partnership that accelerates teacher growth toward the district's instructional goals.

apps.name: Math PBAT: Assess & Give Feedback
apps.description: A project-based math feedback coach that assesses and gives feedback on Math G-PBAT papers based on the NYPSC Rubric.
apps.creator: Arden Katine
apps.usage: High school math teachers use this app to get rubric-aligned feedback on student Math G-PBAT papers before final submission. It serves as a thought partner during the revision process, helping teachers identify where student work meets the NYPSC Rubric expectations and where targeted feedback can push students to strengthen their mathematical reasoning and communication.
apps.impact: Teachers are spending less time decoding the rubric and more time having meaningful feedback conversations with students. The app has helped standardize how PBAT feedback is delivered across classrooms, ensuring students receive consistent, actionable guidance that helps them revise with purpose rather than guessing at what evaluators are looking for.

apps.name: Algebra with Mathias - Next Gen
apps.description: A chatbot that assists students in preparing for their Next Gen Regents Exam with break-it-down and checking-for-understanding questions in high school algebra.
apps.creator: Mary Chavez
apps.usage: High school algebra students use Mathias as a study partner to prepare for the Next Gen Regents Exam. The chatbot breaks down complex problems into smaller steps and checks for understanding along the way, ensuring students aren't just memorizing procedures but actually building the conceptual foundation they need for exam success.
apps.impact: Students who previously crammed formulas before the Regents are now engaging in deeper practice that mirrors the exam's emphasis on reasoning and explanation. The break-it-down approach has given students a study strategy they can rely on independently, reducing test anxiety and building the kind of mathematical confidence that shows up when it counts.

apps.name: IM Assessing & Advancing Questions Assistant
apps.description: An assistant for educators using Illustrative Mathematics. It helps anticipate student pathways and generates targeted assessing and advancing questions to deepen mathematical understanding for teachers.
apps.creator: Aneka Bruce
apps.usage: Math teachers and coaches use this app during lesson planning to anticipate the different ways students might approach a problem and prepare targeted questions in advance. It helps educators think through assessing questions that reveal student understanding and advancing questions that push thinking forward, all grounded in the IM curriculum.
apps.impact: Teachers are showing up to lessons with a deeper understanding of possible student reasoning paths, leading to more responsive and purposeful questioning in the moment. The shift from reactive to proactive questioning has elevated the quality of mathematical discourse in classrooms and given teachers greater confidence in facilitating rich problem-solving discussions.

apps.name: IM Aligned Observation Feedback
apps.description: Transforms classroom observations into actionable, framework-aligned feedback. Connects IM Look Fors with the Danielson Framework, TRU Framework, ICUCARE Equity Framework, UDL Guidelines, and NCTM Effective Mathematics Teaching Practices to help leaders collect meaningful evidence and generate growth-focused feedback.
apps.creator: Kim Melgar
apps.usage: Instructional coaches and school leaders use this tool after classroom observations to translate what they saw into specific, framework-aligned feedback. The app connects IM Look Fors with multiple research-based frameworks — Danielson, TRU, ICUCARE, UDL, and NCTM — so that feedback is both actionable for teachers and aligned to administrative evaluation requirements.
apps.impact: Leaders are delivering observation feedback that feels genuinely developmental rather than evaluative, because the app helps them ground their notes in specific instructional frameworks teachers already know. The multi-framework alignment has bridged the gap between coaching for math-specific growth and meeting district evaluation requirements, making both feel like part of the same conversation.

apps.name: IM AI Data Analysis and Pattern Finder
apps.description: Safely upload student assessment data, classroom observation records, or implementation surveys to discover patterns in mathematical learning and teaching effectiveness.
apps.creator: Vernon David
apps.usage: Math coaches and teachers upload student assessment data, observation notes, and implementation surveys to uncover patterns they might miss manually. The app analyzes the data and surfaces insights about mathematical learning trends and teaching effectiveness, turning raw numbers into actionable next steps for instruction.
apps.impact: Educators are making more informed instructional decisions because they can now see patterns across classrooms and grade levels that were previously invisible in spreadsheets. The data-driven insights have elevated coaching conversations from anecdotal to evidence-based, helping schools target their IM implementation efforts where they matter most.

apps.name: IM Check for Conceptual Understanding
apps.description: A check for understanding resource that helps high school students in Illustrative Math curriculum courses (Algebra 1, Geometry, Algebra 2) assess and deepen their conceptual understanding of lesson content through guided inquiry and feedback.
apps.creator: NYC Solves
apps.usage: High school students in IM courses use this tool after completing a lesson to verify they've grasped the key concepts before moving on to practice problems. Students articulate their understanding in their own words and receive targeted feedback on any misconceptions, making it a self-directed checkpoint that builds confidence and catches gaps early.
apps.impact: Students are catching conceptual misunderstandings before they compound into larger gaps, leading to stronger performance on assessments and more productive practice time. The tool has empowered students to take ownership of their learning by giving them a way to self-assess that goes deeper than checking if they got the right answer.

apps.name: [6-8] NYCPS Student Work Misconception Analyzer
apps.description: Creates question sets or pathways in the moment for teachers to guide grades 6-8 students through problem-solving processes based on their misconceptions, emphasizing open-ended exploration and critical thinking.
apps.creator: Arden Katine
apps.usage: Middle school math teachers analyze student work to identify misconceptions, then use the app to generate targeted question pathways on the spot. These questions guide students from their specific error toward deeper understanding through open-ended exploration rather than simply correcting the mistake, making misconceptions a starting point for learning.
apps.impact: Teachers are responding to student errors with greater precision and intentionality, using misconceptions as diagnostic tools rather than things to fix. Students are developing stronger mathematical reasoning because the question pathways push them to think critically about why their approach didn't work, building the kind of flexible problem-solving skills that transfer across topics.

apps.name: Maryam Flow
apps.description: Helps students improve their math metacognition by guiding them through four types of reflective questions: Understanding (making sense of problems), Planning (mapping out solution steps), Solving (executing steps), and Reviewing (checking accuracy and sense-making).
apps.creator: Varuni Tiruchelvam
apps.usage: Students work through math problems with the app as a metacognitive coach, answering structured prompts that slow down their thinking and make their problem-solving process visible. The four-phase framework — Understanding, Planning, Solving, Reviewing — helps students develop habits of mind that transfer across math topics and grade levels.
apps.impact: Students are becoming more aware of how they approach math problems, moving from impulsive answer-seeking to deliberate, reflective problem solving. Teachers have noticed that students who regularly use the app are better able to articulate where they get stuck and self-correct before asking for help, building the kind of mathematical independence that lasts beyond any single lesson.

apps.impact: Inspired students to see themselves as future engineers — especially those from communities where engineering wasn't presented as a possibility, shifting their self-concept from "that's not for people like me" to "I'm going to build that."

apps.name: Scholarships 101
apps.description: KIPP alumni use Scholarships 101 to discover scholarship opportunities that match their profile and get guided support through the application process.
apps.creator: Edgar Javier Ortiz
apps.usage: KIPP alumni use Scholarships 101 to discover scholarship opportunities that match their profile and get guided support through the application process. The app helps students navigate the often overwhelming landscape of financial aid by breaking down eligibility requirements, deadlines, and essay strategies into manageable steps.
apps.impact: Alumni who previously left scholarship money on the table because the process felt too complex are now submitting stronger, more targeted applications. The app has helped level the playing field for first-generation college students who don't have family networks to guide them through the financial aid landscape.

apps.name: Passion Project Organizer
apps.description: Students use the Passion Project Organizer to identify pockets of free time in their schedules and build a realistic plan for making progress on personal projects they care about.
apps.creator: Sadie Zuniga
apps.usage: Students use the Passion Project Organizer to identify pockets of free time in their schedules and build a realistic plan for making progress on personal projects they care about. The app helps them break big ideas into manageable steps and stay accountable to their own goals with structured time management support.
apps.impact: Students who felt overwhelmed by the gap between their ideas and execution are now making steady progress on passion projects because the app gives them a clear, personalized roadmap. The structured approach has helped students develop time management and self-direction skills that extend well beyond any single project.

apps.name: Postsecondary Pathway Planner
apps.description: Supports high school students in backwards planning their postsecondary pathways, allowing them to explore various careers through assessments and self-reflection.
apps.creator: Peter Gooden
apps.usage: High school students use this tool to explore career options and work backwards from their postsecondary goals to build an actionable plan. Through self-assessments and guided reflection, students identify their strengths and interests, research career pathways, and map out the academic and extracurricular steps they need to take now to get where they want to go.
apps.impact: Students who previously felt uncertain about life after high school are developing a clearer sense of direction and ownership over their future. The backwards planning approach has shifted college and career conversations from abstract aspirations to concrete, personalized roadmaps that students revisit and refine over time.

apps.name: Level Up Helper
apps.description: Supports users in the job application process by helping them build better resumes and cover letters catered to either an industry or a specific job description.
apps.creator: Angelina Lorenzo
apps.usage: Educators and professionals use Level Up Helper when preparing to advance in their careers. The app guides users through tailoring their resumes and cover letters to specific roles or industries, helping them highlight relevant experience and craft compelling narratives that stand out in competitive application pools.
apps.impact: Users who previously felt stuck in the application process are approaching job searches with greater clarity and confidence. The personalized guidance has helped educators in particular translate their classroom and leadership experience into language that resonates across sectors, opening up career pathways they hadn't considered before.

apps.name: STEM Career Explorer
apps.description: A chatbot that guides students through exploring different STEM career paths, providing information on job requirements, salary expectations, and educational pathways for various STEM professions to help students make informed career choices.
apps.creator: Dan Thomas
apps.usage: Students chat with the bot to explore STEM careers that match their interests, learning about what different roles actually involve, what education they need, and what they can expect to earn. The conversational format makes career research feel approachable and personalized rather than like scrolling through a database of job listings.
apps.impact: Students are making more informed decisions about their coursework and postsecondary plans because they now understand the concrete steps between where they are and where they want to go in STEM. The app has been especially powerful for students who didn't see themselves in STEM careers before — hearing about the breadth of options has expanded their sense of what's possible.

apps.name: SummerScope: Summer Program Finder
apps.description: Matches students with practical experiences — summer programs, workshops, training programs, or after-school programs — that suit their unique interests and competencies they wish to develop.
apps.creator: Dale Pilgrim
apps.usage: Students and counselors use SummerScope to find experiential learning opportunities that align with individual interests and skill development goals. The app goes beyond summer camps to surface workshops, training programs, and after-school options, giving students a comprehensive view of how they can build competencies outside the traditional classroom.
apps.impact: Students who previously missed out on enrichment opportunities because they didn't know what was available are now connecting with programs that match their passions and growth areas. The personalized matching has helped counselors scale their advising, ensuring more students access the kinds of hands-on experiences that build both skills and confidence.

apps.name: Summit Career Explorer
apps.description: Allows high school students to explore realistic, day-in-the-life career scenarios from a variety of professional fields and encourages exploration of career resources specific to where users live.
apps.creator: Mike Taubman
apps.usage: Students explore immersive day-in-the-life scenarios across different careers, getting a realistic sense of what various professions actually look like beyond job titles. The app also connects students to career resources in their local area, making exploration feel actionable and grounded in opportunities they can actually pursue.
apps.impact: Students are discovering careers they never knew existed and developing a more nuanced understanding of what different jobs actually involve day to day. The localized resource feature has been especially powerful for students in communities where career exposure is limited, opening doors they didn't know were there.

apps.name: FreeWorld 1:1 Tutoring
apps.description: Designed to help tutor Free Agents to pass their CDL A Commercial Learner's Permit tests at the DMV. Solves for too many students needing 1:1 tutoring with not enough staff.
apps.creator: Allison Carroll
apps.usage: Free Agents preparing for their CDL A Commercial Learner's Permit use this app as a personal tutor to study for the DMV exam. The app fills a critical staffing gap by providing on-demand, individualized test prep that would otherwise require scarce 1:1 coaching time from program staff.
apps.impact: More students are passing their CDL permit tests on the first attempt because they have consistent access to targeted practice and support. The app has freed up coaching staff to focus on hands-on driving instruction and mentorship while ensuring no student falls through the cracks due to limited tutoring availability.

apps.name: College Challenge Coach
apps.description: Helps students think through challenges and obstacles they may encounter in college, building problem-solving skills and resilience before they leave high school.
apps.creator: Andrea Walker
apps.usage: Students work through realistic college scenarios — from navigating roommate conflicts to managing academic setbacks — and practice thinking through strategies before they face these situations for real. Counselors use it as part of college readiness programming to help students build the problem-solving and self-advocacy skills that are often the difference between persisting and dropping out.
apps.impact: Students are arriving at college readiness conversations with a more realistic and empowered mindset about what lies ahead. Rather than being blindsided by challenges, they have already rehearsed how to navigate them, leading to stronger self-advocacy skills and a greater sense of agency about their postsecondary journey.

apps.name: nXu: Exploring Real-Life Career Field Scenarios
apps.description: Allows high school students to explore realistic, day-in-the-life career scenarios from a variety of professional fields and encourages students to explore other careers related to their interests.
apps.creator: Jabali Sawicki
apps.usage: High school students immerse themselves in day-in-the-life scenarios across different careers, experiencing what various professions actually feel like beyond a job title. The app also surfaces related career paths students might not have considered, encouraging broader exploration based on their interests and strengths.
apps.impact: Students are developing a more realistic and nuanced understanding of professional life, moving past surface-level assumptions about what careers involve. The cross-pollination of related career suggestions has been especially powerful for students who felt locked into a single path, helping them see how their interests can lead in multiple rewarding directions.

apps.name: Start My Art
apps.description: Helps middle school students explore career paths in the visual art field. Guides them in selecting a path to research best suited to their unique interests, moving beyond the typical art careers found in web search engines.
apps.creator: Jennifer Nyang
apps.usage: Middle school art students use this bot to discover visual art career paths they didn't know existed. Instead of defaulting to generic search results, students engage in a guided exploration that matches their specific interests to lesser-known but exciting careers in the art world, from concept art to textile design to art conservation.
apps.impact: Students are beginning to see art as a viable career path with real breadth and opportunity, not just painting or graphic design. The personalized exploration has sparked new ambitions in students who previously thought their creative interests couldn't translate into a profession, expanding their vision of what an art career can look like.

apps.name: Project Yourself
apps.description: Designed to source and create projects to provide users with opportunities for practical application of new skills. Built for career changers and seekers who want to upskill and showcase credibility in a demanding job market.
apps.creator: Jacqueline Layreau
apps.usage: Career changers and job seekers use Project Yourself to find and create portfolio-worthy projects that demonstrate real skills to potential employers. The app helps users identify gaps between where they are and where they want to be, then generates practical project ideas that build credibility and showcase their abilities in tangible ways.
apps.impact: Users who felt stuck in career transitions are now building portfolios of work that speak louder than credentials alone. The project-based approach has given career changers a way to prove their capabilities to hiring managers, turning the intimidating process of reinvention into a series of achievable, demonstrable steps.

apps.name: SOS Guide - Summer Opportunities
apps.description: Assists high school students (grades 9-12) in finding and applying to summer programs that match their grade, interests, GPA, and location preferences.
apps.creator: Karen Beckers
apps.usage: High school students use the SOS Guide to search for summer programs tailored to their specific grade level, academic interests, GPA, and location. Instead of sifting through hundreds of options on their own, students get personalized recommendations and guidance on the application process, making summer planning feel manageable rather than overwhelming.
apps.impact: Students who would have otherwise spent their summers without enrichment opportunities are now discovering and applying to programs that align with their goals. Counselors report that the app has democratized access to summer programming, especially for first-generation students who lack the networks that typically surface these opportunities.

apps.usage: Students take quizzes hosted by a comedically angry character who adds personality and humor to knowledge assessment. The app was built by a student, showcasing creative game design skills.
apps.impact: Proved that humor and personality in edtech drives engagement — students literally request this app by name, which is the ultimate test of whether a learning tool actually works.

apps.usage: Students level up by mastering course content in a D&D-inspired adventure where academic knowledge translates directly into character abilities. The RPG mechanics create intrinsic motivation to learn.
apps.impact: Showed that RPG mechanics can drive genuine academic motivation — students grind for knowledge like gamers grind for XP, voluntarily studying to level up their characters.

apps.usage: Students, especially newcomers, discover activities, cultural experiences, and food options across NYC. The tool helps students navigate and enjoy the incredible resources of their city.
apps.impact: Helped students — especially newcomers who feel overwhelmed by the city — discover that NYC is full of free and accessible experiences designed just for them.

apps.name: Project Babel
apps.description: Helps students write the first line of a novel, guiding them through the craft of crafting compelling opening sentences.
apps.creator: Meridith Hinshaw
apps.usage: Students use Project Babel to overcome the blank page paralysis that often stalls creative writing projects. The app guides them through crafting a compelling opening line for their novel, turning the most intimidating part of the writing process into a structured, supportive experience that builds momentum for the rest of their story.
apps.impact: Students who used to stare at empty pages for entire class periods are now generating opening lines they feel genuinely excited about. The focused approach to first lines has taught students that great writing starts with intentional choices, giving them a craft-based mindset that carries through the rest of their creative work.

apps.name: Judy H., Humanities Classroom Funmaker
apps.description: Social studies teachers use Judy H. to quickly generate classroom-ready games and hands-on activities that bring concepts to life.
apps.creator: Nkomo Morris
apps.usage: Social studies teachers use Judy H. to quickly generate classroom-ready games and hands-on activities that bring concepts to life. Instead of spending hours designing engaging review activities, teachers describe the concept they're teaching and get a playable game or activity they can run in class that same day.
apps.impact: Students are retaining social studies content at deeper levels because they're learning through play rather than passive note-taking. Teachers have found that the game-based approach sparks richer class discussions, with students making connections between concepts they might have missed through traditional instruction alone.

apps.name: Bad Idea Bot
apps.description: Students interact with the bot to generate hilariously absurd ideas that become springboards for creative writing.
apps.creator: Carey Swanson
apps.usage: Students interact with the bot to generate hilariously absurd ideas that become springboards for creative writing. Teachers use it as a warm-up or writing prompt generator that gets even the most reluctant writers excited to put pen to paper, turning laughter into the gateway for storytelling and comic creation.
apps.impact: Students who typically resist writing assignments are now eagerly producing stories and comics because the ideas feel fun rather than forced. The low-stakes, humor-driven approach has lowered the barrier to creative expression, helping kids discover that writing can be joyful and that their wildest ideas are worth exploring.

apps.name: Lyrics to Learning
apps.description: Transforms song lyrics into cross-curricular learning opportunities, helping students analyze language, themes, and cultural context through music they already love.
apps.creator: Cornell Carelock
apps.usage: Students bring in songs they listen to and use the app to break down lyrics for literary devices, historical references, and thematic connections across subjects. Teachers use it as a bridge to make ELA and social studies content more relatable by meeting students where their interests already live.
apps.impact: Students who were previously disengaged in traditional literacy activities are now eagerly analyzing text because it feels relevant to their lives. The app has helped teachers unlock new entry points into close reading and critical thinking by leveraging the music students are already passionate about.

apps.name: Passion Project Brainstorm Engine
apps.description: Supports students to develop a Passion Project topic that uses their interests, has a connection to their community, and solves a dilemma that is within their locus of control.
apps.creator: Evan O'Connell
apps.usage: Students use the Brainstorm Engine to discover passion project topics at the intersection of their personal interests, community connections, and actionable problems. The app guides them through a structured brainstorming process that ensures their project idea is both meaningful to them and realistic to execute.
apps.impact: Students are choosing passion project topics with greater intentionality and follow-through because the ideas emerge from their own interests and community context rather than being assigned. The focus on locus of control has helped students select projects they can actually complete, reducing frustration and increasing the quality of final products.

apps.name: Re-Imagining Migration

apps.impact: Equipped educators with the language and strategies to turn classrooms into genuinely welcoming spaces — immigrant students who felt invisible started feeling seen, and their classmates gained empathy and global understanding.

apps.impact: Helped people navigate the nuances of intercultural communication that no textbook can fully capture — the real, messy, beautiful complexity of working across cultures.

apps.name: Equity Minded Inquiry
apps.description: Fairfax students and teachers engage in equity-focused inquiry projects that examine real issues of inclusion, access, and representation in their school community.
apps.usage: Fairfax students and teachers engage in equity-focused inquiry projects that examine real issues of inclusion, access, and representation in their school community. The tool structures the inquiry process so conversations about equity are productive rather than performative.
apps.impact: Pushed Fairfax classrooms toward more inclusive, equity-centered practice through student-led inquiry rather than top-down mandates — when students investigate equity themselves, the insights stick in ways that professional development workshops never achieve.

apps.name: Creating Inclusive Learning Communities
apps.description: This app helps educators serve increasingly diverse student populations by enabling them to create learning communities where all students feel they belong and develop the knowledge, skills, and understanding they need to thrive in a world on the move.
apps.creator: Adam Strom
apps.usage: Educators use this app to reflect on their classroom practices and develop strategies for building inclusive learning environments. It guides teachers through frameworks for understanding diverse student backgrounds and provides actionable ideas for fostering belonging across cultural, linguistic, and experiential differences.
apps.impact: Teachers have reported a shift in how they approach lesson design, moving from a one-size-fits-all model to intentionally centering student identity and experience. Students from historically marginalized backgrounds are showing greater engagement and participation as classrooms become spaces where their stories and perspectives are valued.

apps.name: Social Justice in Action
apps.description: Addy, your Advocate Assistant, guides you through the planning process of your social justice project.
apps.creator: Amy Orm
apps.usage: Students use Addy as a planning partner to design and execute social justice projects in their communities. The app walks them through identifying an issue, researching its root causes, building an action plan, and reflecting on their impact — turning passion for change into structured, achievable advocacy work.
apps.impact: Students are moving from talking about social issues to taking meaningful action, with the scaffolded planning process giving them the confidence and structure to follow through. The app has helped young people see themselves as capable change-makers, transforming abstract discussions about justice into concrete projects that make a difference in their schools and neighborhoods.

apps.name: Build Knowledge and Disrupt Texts 2.0

The app surfaces diverse authors and perspectives, helping educators build knowledge-rich curricula that are both rigorous and representative of the students they serve.
apps.impact: Teachers are moving beyond isolated readings to interconnected text sets that deepen student understanding across disciplines. The emphasis on diverse authorship has transformed classroom libraries and reading lists, ensuring students encounter voices and perspectives that reflect the full range of human experience while building content knowledge.

The emphasis on critical thinking over correct answers has changed the culture of math classrooms, with students more willing to take risks and share their thinking because mistakes have become launching points rather than dead ends.

The app has helped shift the perception of standards-aligned math from a rigid checklist into a flexible toolkit that strengthens real-world problem solving, giving both teachers and students a clearer sense of why the math matters.

apps.impact: Teachers have reported a shift in how they approach lesson design, moving from a one-size-fits-all model to intentionally centering student identity and experience. apps.usage: Students use the app to discover their reading level and then receive custom-generated texts about topics they care about, written at or just above their level and in their home language. It also recommends published books and articles for further reading, creating a personalized literacy pathway that meets students exactly where they are.
apps.impact: Students who felt alienated by grade-level texts are now reading with purpose and interest because the content reflects their passions and meets them at the right level of challenge. The home language support has been transformative for multilingual learners, validating their linguistic identities while building English literacy skills in parallel.

apps.name: DANTE: Daily Accessible News Techbot for Education
apps.description: Chooses the most important technology news story per day and rewrites it to be accessible so that neurodiverse students ages 15-25 can understand the meaning of the article.
apps.creator: Beth Rosenberg
apps.usage: Teachers use DANTE to bring current technology news into their classrooms in a format that is accessible to neurodiverse learners. The app selects the day's most important tech story and rewrites it with clear language, simplified structure, and context that helps students ages 15-25 engage meaningfully with real-world events.
apps.impact: Neurodiverse students who were previously excluded from current events discussions because the source material was too dense are now active participants in classroom conversations about technology and society. The daily cadence has built a routine of engagement with the news that students look forward to, making civic literacy feel achievable and relevant.

apps.name: Get Started With IM360
apps.description: Designed to support teachers, instructional coaches, and school/district leaders in creating equitable, student-centered math classrooms through actionable strategies and sustainable systems with a focus on maintaining coherence and rigor of IM.
apps.creator: Araceli Flores
apps.usage: Teachers, coaches, and district leaders use this app as an onboarding and ongoing support tool for implementing Illustrative Mathematics with fidelity. It provides actionable strategies for maintaining the coherence and rigor of IM while adapting instruction to meet the needs of diverse learners, helping teams build sustainable systems for equitable math classrooms.
apps.impact: Schools that previously struggled with inconsistent IM implementation are finding common ground through the app's structured guidance. Leaders and coaches report that it has accelerated the shift from compliance-driven curriculum adoption to genuine ownership, with teachers embracing student-centered practices because they understand the why behind the instructional moves.

apps.name: LE - Teaching for Equity Coach
apps.description: Teachers describe a specific classroom challenge and the coach draws on Teaching for Equity research to offer grounded, practical strategies.
apps.creator: Chong Hao Fu
apps.usage: Teachers describe a specific classroom challenge and the coach draws on Teaching for Equity research to offer grounded, practical strategies. It acts as an on-demand thought partner that helps educators connect their daily struggles to evidence-based equity practices, making research feel actionable rather than abstract.
apps.impact: Teachers are bridging the gap between equity research and classroom practice in real time, applying strategies they might never have discovered on their own. The coaching model has made equity-focused professional growth feel continuous and responsive rather than limited to scheduled training sessions.

apps.name: Foreign Language Study Strategies
apps.description: Provides students with personalized and accessible support to enhance their Spanish language learning experience through interactive assistance and guidance to help them improve on their foreign language acquisition.
apps.creator: Henry Columna
apps.usage: Spanish language students use this app as a personalized study companion that provides interactive guidance tailored to their current level and learning goals. The app offers targeted strategies for vocabulary building, grammar practice, and conversational fluency, giving students on-demand support outside of class time when they need it most.
apps.impact: Students are developing stronger language acquisition habits because they have a tool that meets them at their level and adapts to their needs. The personalized approach has been especially valuable for students who feel self-conscious practicing in class, giving them a low-pressure space to build confidence before engaging in live conversation.

apps.name: FreeWorld 1:1 Tutoring
apps.description: Designed to help tutor Free Agents to pass their CDL A Commercial Learner's Permit tests at the DMV. Solves for too many students needing 1:1 tutoring with not enough staff.
apps.creator: Allison Carroll
apps.usage: Free Agents preparing for their CDL A Commercial Learner's Permit use this app as a personal tutor to study for the DMV exam. The app fills a critical staffing gap by providing on-demand, individualized test prep that would otherwise require scarce 1:1 coaching time from program staff.
apps.impact: More students are passing their CDL permit tests on the first attempt because they have consistent access to targeted practice and support. The app has freed up coaching staff to focus on hands-on driving instruction and mentorship while ensuring no student falls through the cracks due to limited tutoring availability.

apps.name: MathMood
apps.description: An SEL Math Integration app designed to support math educators from Pre-K to 12th grade in seamlessly integrating Social and Emotional Learning practices into their daily math lessons. Provides personalized SEL strategies, resources, and real-time in-lesson guidance tailored to specific grade levels and math topics.
apps.creator: Renee Davis
apps.usage: Math teachers across grade levels use MathMood to find SEL strategies that fit naturally into the math content they are already teaching. The app provides tailored resources, in-lesson prompts, and reflection tools that help educators address students' emotional needs while maintaining rigorous math instruction, turning everyday lessons into opportunities to build both mathematical and social-emotional skills.
apps.impact: Teachers report that students are more willing to engage with challenging math problems when the classroom environment intentionally supports emotional safety and self-regulation. By weaving SEL into math rather than treating it as a separate initiative, educators have seen stronger student persistence, more collaborative problem-solving, and a shift in how students talk about mistakes as part of learning.

apps.name: UDL Matters
apps.description: Creates access points to specific learning objectives for all students through Universal Design for Learning. Solves the classroom dilemma of teachers' ability to meet students where they're at.
apps.creator: Chelsea Grantham
apps.usage: Teachers input their learning objectives and the app generates multiple access points using Universal Design for Learning principles. It helps educators quickly design lessons that offer varied means of engagement, representation, and action so that every student — regardless of ability or learning style — can meaningfully access the content.
apps.impact: Teachers who previously struggled to differentiate for diverse learners are now designing more inclusive lessons from the start rather than retrofitting accommodations after the fact. The UDL framework has shifted the conversation from what students can't do to how instruction can be designed so all students can succeed.

apps.name: Brookline Equity Coach
apps.description: The Brookline Equity Coach provides AI-powered support to teachers so that they can promote belonging for all students. The tool is aligned to the district's new strategy as well as the revised Massachusetts Classroom Teacher Rubric.
apps.creator: Ruiz Clark
apps.usage: Teachers use the Equity Coach to get targeted feedback on their instructional practices through the lens of equity and belonging. The app helps them reflect on lesson plans, classroom interactions, and assessment strategies, offering suggestions aligned to the district's equity framework and the Massachusetts teacher evaluation rubric.
apps.impact: Educators are approaching equity work with greater confidence and specificity, moving beyond abstract principles to concrete, actionable shifts in their practice. The alignment to both district strategy and state evaluation standards has made equity-centered teaching feel less like an add-on and more like an integrated part of professional growth.

apps.name: [6-8] NYCPS Student Work Misconception Analyzer
apps.description: Creates question sets or pathways in the moment for teachers to guide grades 6-8 students through problem-solving processes based on their misconceptions, emphasizing open-ended exploration and critical thinking.
apps.creator: Arden Katine
apps.usage: Middle school math teachers analyze student work to identify misconceptions, then use the app to generate targeted question pathways on the spot. These questions guide students from their specific error toward deeper understanding through open-ended exploration rather than simply correcting the mistake, making misconceptions a starting point for learning.
apps.impact: Teachers are responding to student errors with greater precision and intentionality, using misconceptions as diagnostic tools rather than things to fix. Students are developing stronger mathematical reasoning because the question pathways push them to think critically about why their approach didn't work, building the kind of flexible problem-solving skills that transfer across topics.

apps.name: Maryam Flow
apps.description: Helps students improve their math metacognition by guiding them through four types of reflective questions: Understanding (making sense of problems), Planning (mapping out solution steps), Solving (executing steps), and Reviewing (checking accuracy and sense-making).
apps.creator: Varuni Tiruchelvam
apps.usage: Students work through math problems with the app as a metacognitive coach, answering structured prompts that slow down their thinking and make their problem-solving process visible. The four-phase framework — Understanding, Planning, Solving, Reviewing — helps students develop habits of mind that transfer across math topics and grade levels.
apps.impact: Students are becoming more aware of how they approach math problems, moving from impulsive answer-seeking to deliberate, reflective problem solving. Teachers have noticed that students who regularly use the app are better able to articulate where they get stuck and self-correct before asking for help, building the kind of mathematical independence that lasts beyond any single lesson.

apps.impact: Transformed lesson planning from hours of manual work into minutes of guided generation. Every lesson is automatically accessible for SPED and multilingual learners, connected to DC students' lived experiences, and aligned to Kingsman's competency-based framework. Made individualized, high-quality instruction the default.

apps.name: Mindful Mastery
apps.description: Fairfax students practice mindfulness and stress management techniques through guided exercises and reflections.
apps.usage: Fairfax students practice mindfulness and stress management techniques through guided exercises and reflections. The tool provides concrete strategies for managing the pressure of a competitive academic environment, giving students skills they can use both in school and at home.

apps.name: Concavity
apps.description: Designed to teach changing rates of change by walking through the specific steps for solving an example submitted by a high school math student.
apps.creator: Kenneth Dearden
apps.usage: High school math students submit their own concavity problems and the app walks them through each step of the solution process. Rather than presenting a generic worked example, it meets students where they are by using their specific problem as the teaching vehicle, making the abstract concept of changing rates of change feel concrete and personal.
apps.impact: Students who previously struggled to connect the idea of concavity to the mechanics of solving problems are now building intuition through guided practice with their own examples. The step-by-step approach has helped demystify one of calculus's more conceptually challenging topics, giving students the confidence to tackle related problems independently.

apps.name: Postsecondary Pathway Planner
apps.description: Supports high school students in backwards planning their postsecondary pathways, allowing them to explore various careers through assessments and self-reflection.
apps.creator: Peter Gooden
apps.usage: High school students use this tool to explore career options and work backwards from their postsecondary goals to build an actionable plan. Through self-assessments and guided reflection, students identify their strengths and interests, research career pathways, and map out the academic and extracurricular steps they need to take now to get where they want to go.
apps.impact: Students who previously felt uncertain about life after high school are developing a clearer sense of direction and ownership over their future. The backwards planning approach has shifted college and career conversations from abstract aspirations to concrete, personalized roadmaps that students revisit and refine over time.

apps.name: Have I Got a Book for YOU!
apps.description: High school students share their reading preferences, interests, and past favorites with the app and receive personalized book recommendations that match their tastes.
apps.creator: Amanda Schear
apps.usage: High school students share their reading preferences, interests, and past favorites with the app and receive personalized book recommendations that match their tastes. Teachers use it to help students find their next read during independent reading time, eliminating the common barrier of students not knowing what to pick up next.
apps.impact: Students who described themselves as non-readers are now voluntarily picking up books because the recommendations feel personally curated rather than assigned. The app has helped teachers move from a one-size-fits-all reading list to a culture of choice reading where every student has a book that speaks to them.

apps.name: DANTE: Daily Accessible News Techbot for Education
apps.description: Chooses the most important technology news story per day and rewrites it to be accessible so that neurodiverse students ages 15-25 can understand the meaning of the article.
apps.creator: Beth Rosenberg
apps.usage: Teachers use DANTE to bring current technology news into their classrooms in a format that is accessible to neurodiverse learners. The app selects the day's most important tech story and rewrites it with clear language, simplified structure, and context that helps students ages 15-25 engage meaningfully with real-world events.
apps.impact: Neurodiverse students who were previously excluded from current events discussions because the source material was too dense are now active participants in classroom conversations about technology and society. The daily cadence has built a routine of engagement with the news that students look forward to, making civic literacy feel achievable and relevant.

apps.name: STEM Career Explorer
apps.description: A chatbot that guides students through exploring different STEM career paths, providing information on job requirements, salary expectations, and educational pathways for various STEM professions to help students make informed career choices.
apps.creator: Dan Thomas
apps.usage: Students chat with the bot to explore STEM careers that match their interests, learning about what different roles actually involve, what education they need, and what they can expect to earn. The conversational format makes career research feel approachable and personalized rather than like scrolling through a database of job listings.
apps.impact: Students are making more informed decisions about their coursework and postsecondary plans because they now understand the concrete steps between where they are and where they want to go in STEM. The app has been especially powerful for students who didn't see themselves in STEM careers before — hearing about the breadth of options has expanded their sense of what's possible.

apps.name: FAFSA Assistant
apps.description: Parents and families use this bot as a step-by-step guide through the FAFSA application process.
apps.creator: Anthony Lifieri
apps.usage: Parents and families use this bot as a step-by-step guide through the FAFSA application process. It breaks down confusing financial aid terminology, clarifies which documents are needed, and walks families through each section of the form so they can complete it with confidence rather than abandoning it out of frustration.
apps.impact: Families who previously left financial aid on the table because the FAFSA felt too intimidating are now completing applications successfully. The app has been especially valuable for first-generation college families who lack the support networks that typically guide students through the financial aid process.

apps.name: StudyPal
apps.description: Helps students explain the steps involved in solving numerical problems in Physics and other subject areas during self study.
apps.creator: Will John Odiong
apps.usage: Students use StudyPal during independent study to work through numerical problems step by step. Rather than just giving answers, the app prompts students to articulate their reasoning at each stage, helping them build problem-solving fluency in physics and math by learning to explain their own thinking process.
apps.impact: Students who used to get stuck and give up on multi-step problems are now persisting through challenges because the app breaks down the process into manageable pieces. The emphasis on self-explanation has deepened conceptual understanding, with students transferring problem-solving strategies across subjects rather than memorizing isolated procedures.

apps.name: Math PBAT: Assess & Give Feedback
apps.description: A project-based math feedback coach that assesses and gives feedback on Math G-PBAT papers based on the NYPSC Rubric.
apps.creator: Arden Katine
apps.usage: High school math teachers use this app to get rubric-aligned feedback on student Math G-PBAT papers before final submission. It serves as a thought partner during the revision process, helping teachers identify where student work meets the NYPSC Rubric expectations and where targeted feedback can push students to strengthen their mathematical reasoning and communication.
apps.impact: Teachers are spending less time decoding the rubric and more time having meaningful feedback conversations with students. The app has helped standardize how PBAT feedback is delivered across classrooms, ensuring students receive consistent, actionable guidance that helps them revise with purpose rather than guessing at what evaluators are looking for.

apps.name: SummerScope: Summer Program Finder
apps.description: Matches students with practical experiences — summer programs, workshops, training programs, or after-school programs — that suit their unique interests and competencies they wish to develop.
apps.creator: Dale Pilgrim
apps.usage: Students and counselors use SummerScope to find experiential learning opportunities that align with individual interests and skill development goals. The app goes beyond summer camps to surface workshops, training programs, and after-school options, giving students a comprehensive view of how they can build competencies outside the traditional classroom.
apps.impact: Students who previously missed out on enrichment opportunities because they didn't know what was available are now connecting with programs that match their passions and growth areas. The personalized matching has helped counselors scale their advising, ensuring more students access the kinds of hands-on experiences that build both skills and confidence.

apps.name: Summit Career Explorer
apps.description: Allows high school students to explore realistic, day-in-the-life career scenarios from a variety of professional fields and encourages exploration of career resources specific to where users live.
apps.creator: Mike Taubman
apps.usage: Students explore immersive day-in-the-life scenarios across different careers, getting a realistic sense of what various professions actually look like beyond job titles. The app also connects students to career resources in their local area, making exploration feel actionable and grounded in opportunities they can actually pursue.
apps.impact: Students are discovering careers they never knew existed and developing a more nuanced understanding of what different jobs actually involve day to day. The localized resource feature has been especially powerful for students in communities where career exposure is limited, opening doors they didn't know were there.

apps.name: Algebra with Mathias - Next Gen
apps.description: A chatbot that assists students in preparing for their Next Gen Regents Exam with break-it-down and checking-for-understanding questions in high school algebra.
apps.creator: Mary Chavez
apps.usage: High school algebra students use Mathias as a study partner to prepare for the Next Gen Regents Exam. The chatbot breaks down complex problems into smaller steps and checks for understanding along the way, ensuring students aren't just memorizing procedures but actually building the conceptual foundation they need for exam success.
apps.impact: Students who previously crammed formulas before the Regents are now engaging in deeper practice that mirrors the exam's emphasis on reasoning and explanation. The break-it-down approach has given students a study strategy they can rely on independently, reducing test anxiety and building the kind of mathematical confidence that shows up when it counts.

apps.name: College Challenge Coach
apps.description: Helps students think through challenges and obstacles they may encounter in college, building problem-solving skills and resilience before they leave high school.
apps.creator: Andrea Walker
apps.usage: Students work through realistic college scenarios — from navigating roommate conflicts to managing academic setbacks — and practice thinking through strategies before they face these situations for real. Counselors use it as part of college readiness programming to help students build the problem-solving and self-advocacy skills that are often the difference between persisting and dropping out.
apps.impact: Students are arriving at college readiness conversations with a more realistic and empowered mindset about what lies ahead. Rather than being blindsided by challenges, they have already rehearsed how to navigate them, leading to stronger self-advocacy skills and a greater sense of agency about their postsecondary journey.

apps.name: nXu: Exploring Real-Life Career Field Scenarios
apps.description: Allows high school students to explore realistic, day-in-the-life career scenarios from a variety of professional fields and encourages students to explore other careers related to their interests.
apps.creator: Jabali Sawicki
apps.usage: High school students immerse themselves in day-in-the-life scenarios across different careers, experiencing what various professions actually feel like beyond a job title. The app also surfaces related career paths students might not have considered, encouraging broader exploration based on their interests and strengths.
apps.impact: Students are developing a more realistic and nuanced understanding of professional life, moving past surface-level assumptions about what careers involve. The cross-pollination of related career suggestions has been especially powerful for students who felt locked into a single path, helping them see how their interests can lead in multiple rewarding directions.

apps.name: IM Check for Conceptual Understanding
apps.description: A check for understanding resource that helps high school students in Illustrative Math curriculum courses (Algebra 1, Geometry, Algebra 2) assess and deepen their conceptual understanding of lesson content through guided inquiry and feedback.
apps.creator: NYC Solves
apps.usage: High school students in IM courses use this tool after completing a lesson to verify they've grasped the key concepts before moving on to practice problems. Students articulate their understanding in their own words and receive targeted feedback on any misconceptions, making it a self-directed checkpoint that builds confidence and catches gaps early.
apps.impact: Students are catching conceptual misunderstandings before they compound into larger gaps, leading to stronger performance on assessments and more productive practice time. The tool has empowered students to take ownership of their learning by giving them a way to self-assess that goes deeper than checking if they got the right answer.

apps.name: SOS Guide - Summer Opportunities
apps.description: Assists high school students (grades 9-12) in finding and applying to summer programs that match their grade, interests, GPA, and location preferences.
apps.creator: Karen Beckers
apps.usage: High school students use the SOS Guide to search for summer programs tailored to their specific grade level, academic interests, GPA, and location. Instead of sifting through hundreds of options on their own, students get personalized recommendations and guidance on the application process, making summer planning feel manageable rather than overwhelming.
apps.impact: Students who would have otherwise spent their summers without enrichment opportunities are now discovering and applying to programs that align with their goals. Counselors report that the app has democratized access to summer programming, especially for first-generation students who lack the networks that typically surface these opportunities.

apps.description: Helps students find and explore a leader who inspires them, then prepare to write about that leader in class.

apps.usage: Students use the app at home to identify a leader they admire — from public figures to family members. The app helps them develop interview questions, research their leader, and organize their thinking before writing an in-class essay without devices.
apps.impact: Students arrive to class more prepared and less afraid to write. The app does the cognitive heavy lifting outside class so the in-class writing is authentically theirs.

apps.name: Final Course Reflection
apps.description: Guides students through deep personal reflection on failure, leadership, uncertainty, and growth throughout the course.
apps.creator: Gabriela Caso de los Cobos Tapia
apps.usage: Students engage with probing questions about their experiences, values, and goals. The app pushes them to think critically about what they share — making reflections inherently personal and tied to their own context, which makes generic AI output far less appealing.
apps.impact: Decreased instructor workload while increasing authenticity. Reading genuine app conversations replaced the frustration of evaluating potentially AI-generated essays.

apps.name: Business Math Tutor
apps.description: College students studying business get help with the math requirements they didn't expect when they chose their major. The tool breaks down business calculations, statistics, and financial math into approachable steps.
apps.usage: College students studying business get help with the math requirements they didn't expect when they chose their major. The tool breaks down business calculations, statistics, and financial math into approachable steps.
apps.impact: Helped business students who chose their major to avoid math discover that business IS math — and gave them the patient support to not let quantitative requirements derail their career goals.

apps.name: Riverside City College Admin Bot
apps.description: Riverside City College students get answers about enrollment, financial aid, campus services, and academic advising without waiting in lines or navigating phone trees.
apps.usage: Riverside City College students get answers about enrollment, financial aid, campus services, and academic advising without waiting in lines or navigating phone trees. The bot handles the routine questions that overwhelm student services offices.
apps.impact: Reduced the administrative barriers that prevent community college students from accessing services they need — students who would have given up after being put on hold now get instant answers.

apps.name: Financing Ventures - TerraCycle Case Study
apps.description: Students prepare at home by exploring different investor roles — venture capitalists, angel investors, corporate investors, family members — each with different motivations and risk tolerances.
apps.creator: Gabriela Caso de los Cobos Tapia
apps.usage: Students prepare at home by exploring different investor roles — venture capitalists, angel investors, corporate investors, family members — each with different motivations and risk tolerances. The students who prepared more with the app performed notably better in the live role-play.
apps.impact: Made the gap between preparation and performance visible. Students who engaged deeply with the app showed measurably stronger understanding during the live simulation.

apps.name: Needham High College List Generator 2.0

apps.impact: Gave every Needham High student the kind of personalized college list guidance that used to require an expensive private counselor — democratizing one of the most consequential decisions of their young lives.

apps.name: Financial Therapist Trainer
apps.description: Students preparing for careers in financial therapy practice counseling conversations with simulated clients who have complex financial and emotional situations.
apps.usage: Students preparing for careers in financial therapy practice counseling conversations with simulated clients who have complex financial and emotional situations. The realistic scenarios build the interpersonal skills needed before working with real clients.
apps.impact: Prepared future financial therapists with the kind of realistic practice that builds confidence before the stakes are real — students learned to navigate the emotional complexity of money conversations in a safe environment.

apps.name: Business Idea

apps.usage: Students develop and refine business ideas, moving from vague concepts to structured business plans they could actually pursue. The tool pushes students to think through market need, feasibility, and competitive advantage.
apps.impact: Turned abstract entrepreneurship concepts into concrete plans students could actually act on — several students have taken ideas generated in this tool and started pursuing them for real.

apps.name: Dreamers & Builders

apps.usage: Aspiring engineers explore career paths, learn about different engineering disciplines, and brainstorm projects that connect their interests to real-world engineering challenges. The tool is specifically designed to reach students from backgrounds underrepresented in STEM.
apps.impact: Inspired students to see themselves as future engineers — especially those from communities where engineering wasn't presented as a possibility, shifting their self-concept from "that's not for people like me" to "I'm going to build that."

apps.name: Exam 1 Reflection College Algebra

apps.usage: College algebra students reflect on their exam performance, identifying patterns in their mistakes and developing specific strategies for improvement. The tool turns a disappointing grade into a learning opportunity with a concrete path forward.
apps.impact: Turned exam failure from a dead end into a turning point — students who used to see a bad grade as confirmation they "can't do math" started seeing it as diagnostic information about exactly what they need to practice next.

The structured framework has given teams a shared language for evaluating trade-offs, leading to stronger alignment and more confident decision-making in high-stakes moments.

apps.impact: Lead teachers are entering tough conversations with greater skill and confidence because they have rehearsed multiple scenarios and received targeted coaching feedback. The role-play approach has accelerated leadership development in ways that traditional PD workshops cannot, helping lead teachers become more effective change agents within their teams.

The app provides on-demand guidance and resources tailored to the community college context, helping bridge gaps in support for students navigating technical coursework.
apps.impact: Students who previously struggled to get timely help outside of office hours now have a reliable resource that meets them where they are. The app has extended the reach of faculty support, ensuring more students stay on track in their IT coursework without falling behind.

apps.creator: Andrea Walker
apps.usage: Students work through realistic college scenarios — from navigating roommate conflicts to managing academic setbacks — and practice thinking through strategies before they face these situations for real. apps.impact: Students are arriving at college readiness conversations with a more realistic and empowered mindset about what lies ahead. Rather than being blindsided by challenges, they have already rehearsed how to navigate them, leading to stronger self-advocacy skills and a greater sense of agency about their postsecondary journey.

apps.impact: Teacher teams are producing more cohesive and challenging units because the evaluation framework pushes them beyond surface-level alignment to deep integration of rigor and relevance. The collaborative review process has strengthened team culture around instructional quality, with educators holding each other accountable to high standards.

apps.impact: Teachers have found that students engage more deeply with IM content when it's woven into projects they care about. The app has helped shift the perception of standards-aligned math from a rigid checklist into a flexible toolkit that strengthens real-world problem solving, giving both teachers and students a clearer sense of why the math matters.

apps.usage: Teachers, coaches, and district leaders use this app as an onboarding and ongoing support tool for implementing Illustrative Mathematics with fidelity. It provides actionable strategies for maintaining the coherence and rigor of IM while adapting instruction to meet the needs of diverse learners, helping teams build sustainable systems for equitable math classrooms.
apps.impact: Schools that previously struggled with inconsistent IM implementation are finding common ground through the app's structured guidance. Leaders and coaches report that it has accelerated the shift from compliance-driven curriculum adoption to genuine ownership, with teachers embracing student-centered practices because they understand the why behind the instructional moves.

apps.name: IM Math Standard Unpacking Coach
apps.description: Helps educators unpack Common Core State Standards and Illustrative Mathematics curricula. Teachers enter a grade level and standard (or topic) and receive a detailed breakdown of what the standard requires and how it connects to IM lessons.
apps.creator: Emily Shriber
apps.usage: Instructional coaches and math teachers use this app to quickly break down what a standard actually demands of students and how Illustrative Mathematics lessons address it. It serves as a planning companion during PLC meetings and lesson prep, helping educators move from surface-level standard references to deep understanding of the mathematical progressions.
apps.impact: Teachers who previously felt overwhelmed by the density of CCSS math standards are now approaching lesson planning with clarity about what students need to know and be able to do. Coaching conversations have become more focused and productive, with educators spending less time decoding standards language and more time designing instruction that hits the mark.

apps.name: IM Assessing & Advancing Questions Assistant
apps.description: An assistant for educators using Illustrative Mathematics. It helps anticipate student pathways and generates targeted assessing and advancing questions to deepen mathematical understanding for teachers.
apps.creator: Aneka Bruce
apps.usage: Math teachers and coaches use this app during lesson planning to anticipate the different ways students might approach a problem and prepare targeted questions in advance. It helps educators think through assessing questions that reveal student understanding and advancing questions that push thinking forward, all grounded in the IM curriculum.
apps.impact: Teachers are showing up to lessons with a deeper understanding of possible student reasoning paths, leading to more responsive and purposeful questioning in the moment. The shift from reactive to proactive questioning has elevated the quality of mathematical discourse in classrooms and given teachers greater confidence in facilitating rich problem-solving discussions.

apps.name: IM Aligned Observation Feedback
apps.description: Transforms classroom observations into actionable, framework-aligned feedback. Connects IM Look Fors with the Danielson Framework, TRU Framework, ICUCARE Equity Framework, UDL Guidelines, and NCTM Effective Mathematics Teaching Practices to help leaders collect meaningful evidence and generate growth-focused feedback.
apps.creator: Kim Melgar
apps.usage: Instructional coaches and school leaders use this tool after classroom observations to translate what they saw into specific, framework-aligned feedback. The app connects IM Look Fors with multiple research-based frameworks — Danielson, TRU, ICUCARE, UDL, and NCTM — so that feedback is both actionable for teachers and aligned to administrative evaluation requirements.
apps.impact: Leaders are delivering observation feedback that feels genuinely developmental rather than evaluative, because the app helps them ground their notes in specific instructional frameworks teachers already know. The multi-framework alignment has bridged the gap between coaching for math-specific growth and meeting district evaluation requirements, making both feel like part of the same conversation.

apps.name: IM AI Data Analysis and Pattern Finder

The app analyzes the data and surfaces insights about mathematical learning trends and teaching effectiveness, turning raw numbers into actionable next steps for instruction.
apps.impact: Educators are making more informed instructional decisions because they can now see patterns across classrooms and grade levels that were previously invisible in spreadsheets. The data-driven insights have elevated coaching conversations from anecdotal to evidence-based, helping schools target their IM implementation efforts where they matter most.

apps.impact: Students are catching conceptual misunderstandings before they compound into larger gaps, leading to stronger performance on assessments and more productive practice time. The tool has empowered students to take ownership of their learning by giving them a way to self-assess that goes deeper than checking if they got the right answer.
